front: Philosopher: the original fragment behind his most famous aphorism more precisely states that 'upon those who step into the same rivers, different and again different waters flow'
work: Doctrine of Flux and the River

front: Philosopher: this radical disciple of Heraclitus strengthened the river claim to the nonsensical position that one cannot step in the same river even once, and advocates finger-wagging over speech in his namesake Platonic dialogue
work: Doctrine of Flux and the River

front: Work: in this Platonic dialogue, Socrates argues that Heraclitus's doctrine of flux renders knowledge through perception impossible; the 'dice paradox' also appears here, discussing both Heraclitus and Protagoras
work: Doctrine of Flux and the River

front: Philosopher: in the final paragraph of Fear and Trembling, Kierkegaard uses this thinker's river statement and a later refinement of it to illustrate the philosophical impulse to 'go further'
work: Doctrine of Flux and the River

front: Philosopher: in 'A New Refutation of Time,' Borges praises this thinker's river statement's 'dialectical skill'
work: Doctrine of Flux and the River

front: Philosopher: illustrated the unity of opposites through the tension in a bow and a lyre, an analogy that titles an Octavio Paz book
Philosopher: this thinker's statement that 'the way up and the way down are one and the same' exemplifies his concept of the unity of opposites
Philosopher: illustrated the unity of opposites by noting that sea water is 'the purest and most polluted water,' good for fish but poisonous to humans
Philosopher: championed the unity of strife and justice, stating that 'war is common' and 'strife is justice'
Philosopher: his 'downward path' describes fire transforming to water transforming to earth, and vice versa on the 'upward path'
Philosopher: stated that 'for souls it is death to become water' and advised having a 'dry' soul
Philosopher: stated that 'lightning steers the universe,' consistent with his belief that fire was the fundamental element
Philosopher: promoted the idea of ekpyrosis, or cycles of conflagration and fire
Philosopher: first thinker to use the word logos for a general organizing principle governing the universe
Philosopher: stated that logos is 'common, though most people live as if they had their own private understanding' of it
Philosopher: Martin Heidegger delivered an early lecture on this thinker's principle of logos

Philosopher: Hegel stated in Lectures on the History of Philosophy that 'there is no proposition of [this thinker] which I have not adopted in my Logic' and considered him the teacher of both Hippocrates and Plato

Philosopher: in Twilight of the Idols, Nietzsche wrote that this thinker will remain 'eternally right' for 'declaring that Being was an empty illusion'; in Ecce Homo, Nietzsche feels 'warmest' near him because he affirms destruction
Philosopher: Heidegger and Eugen Fink held a seminar at the University of Freiburg on this thinker; Heidegger also glossed his term aletheia as 'disclosure' of truth
Philosopher: Georges Bataille wrote a meditation titled for this thinker beginning 'I myself am War,' published in Acephale as part of 'The Practice of Joy Before Death'

Philosopher: in the School of Athens, Michelangelo was the model for Raphael's depiction of this philosopher, who wears large boots and uses a marble slab as his writing desk apart from the other thinkers
Philosopher: Val Plumwood adopted a view of the food chain named for this Pre-Socratic after a near-fatal crocodile attack

Philosopher: according to Diogenes Laertius, this thinker deposited his writings at a temple of Artemis hoping that familiarity would 'breed contempt,' and once fled society to live alone in the mountains
Philosopher: this Pre-Socratic's surviving fragments make up chapter 22 of the Diels-Kranz numbering system
Philosopher: described a drunk man as being 'led along, stumbling, by a beardless boy' while his soul is moist
Philosopher: Fragment B119, 'ethos anthropoi daimon,' is variously translated as 'a man's character is his fate'
Philosopher: stated that 'nature loves to hide'
Philosopher: said that Homer should have been 'beaten with a staff'
Philosopher: stated that 'the most beautiful kosmos is a pile of things poured out at random'
Philosopher: this thinker's statement that 'eternity is a child playing at the table' appears in the Derveni Papyrus, which also likens him to Orpheus
